content:
# Judges Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_275.jpg, JDG_page_276.jpg

### Gideon Defeats the Midianites

1. Early in the morning, Jerubbaal (that is, Gideon) and his army got up and camped beside the spring of Harod. The Midianite camp was to the north, in the valley near the hill of Moreh.

2. The LORD said to Gideon, "You have too many warriors with me. If I let all of you fight the Midianites, the Israelites will boast to me that they saved themselves by their own strength.

3. So announce to the people, 'Whoever is timid or afraid may leave Mount Gilead and go home.'" So 22,000 of them went home, leaving only 10,000 who were willing to fight.

4. But the LORD told Gideon, "There are still too many! Bring them down to the water, and I will test them. Then I will tell you who will go with you and who will not."

5. When Gideon took his warriors down to the water, the LORD told him, "Divide the men into two groups. In one group put all those who cup water in their hands and lap it up with their tongues like dogs. In the other group put all those who kneel down and drink with their mouths in the stream."

6. Only 300 of the men drank from their hands. All the others got down on their knees and drank with their mouths in the stream.

7. The LORD told Gideon, "With these 300 men I will rescue you and give you victory over the Midianites. Send all the others home."

8. So Gideon collected the provisions and rams' horns of the other warriors and sent them home. But he kept the 300 men with him. The Midianite camp was below him in the valley.

9. That night the LORD said, "Get up! Go down and attack the Midianite camp, for I have given you victory over them!

10. But if you are afraid to attack, go down to the camp with your servant Purah.

11. Listen to what the Midianites are saying, and you will be greatly encouraged. Then you will be eager to attack." So Gideon took his servant Purah and crept down toward the enemy camp.

12. The armies of Midian, Amalek, and the people of the east had settled in the valley like a swarm of locusts. Their camels were like grains of sand on the seashore — too many to count!

13. Gideon crept up just as a man was telling his companion about a dream. The man said, "I had this dream, and in my dream a loaf of barley bread came tumbling down into the Midianite camp. It hit a tent, and the tent collapsed and turned upside down, and the tent lay flat!"

14. His companion said, "Your dream can only mean one thing — the sword of Gideon son of Joash, the Israelite. God has given him victory over Midian and all its allies!"

15. When Gideon heard the dream and its interpretation, he bowed in worship before the LORD. Then he returned to the Israelite camp and shouted, "Get up! For the LORD has given you victory over the Midianite hordes!"

16. He divided the 300 men into three groups and gave each man a ram's horn and a clay jar with a burning torch inside.

17. Then he said to them, "Keep your eyes on me. When I get to the edge of the camp, do exactly as I do.

18. As soon as I and those with me blow our horns, blow yours all around the camp, and shout, 'For the LORD and for Gideon!'"

19. It was just after midnight, after the changing of the guard, when Gideon and the 100 men with him reached the edge of the Midianite camp. Then they blew their rams' horns and broke their clay jars.

20. Then all three groups blew their horns and broke their jars. They held the blazing torches in their left hands and the horns in their right hands, and they all shouted, "A sword for the LORD and for Gideon!"

21. Each man stood at his position around the camp and watched as all the Midianites rushed around in a panic, shouting as they ran to escape.

22. When the 300 Israelites blew their rams' horns, the LORD caused the warriors in the camp to fight against each other with their swords. Those who were not killed fled to places as far as Beth-shittah near Zererah and to the border of Abel-meholah near Tabbath.

23. Then the men of Israel were called out from Naphtali, Asher, and all of Manasseh, and they chased the Midianites.

24. Gideon also sent messengers throughout the hill country of Ephraim, saying, "Come down and cut off the Midianites! Guard the shallow crossings of the Jordan River as far as Beth-barah." So all the men of Ephraim did as they were told.

25. They captured Oreb and Zeeb, the two Midianite commanders, and killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb, and Zeeb at the winepress of Zeeb. And they continued to chase the Midianites. Afterward the Ephraimites brought the heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon, who was across the Jordan.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The water test (vv.4-7):** Much ink has been spilled over why God chose the lappers over the kneelers. Popular tradition says the lappers were more "alert" or "vigilant." But the text gives no reason — and that's the point. The selection is arbitrary, just as the overall reduction (from 32,000 to 300) is designed to make victory impossible by human calculation.

**The dream (vv.13-14):** Barley bread was the cheapest, most common food — peasant bread. The dream's symbolism is perfect: the weakest, humblest thing (a loaf of barley bread) demolishes the mightiest (a Midianite tent). Even Midian's own soldiers can see what's coming.

**"A sword for the LORD and for Gideon!" (v.20):** The 300 had no swords. Their weapons were rams' horns, clay jars, and torches — the opposite of military equipment. The "sword" in their battle cry belongs to God alone. The human role is noise and light; the divine role is the actual fighting.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
